test_case.question: Tell me about a time you handled a disagreement between two team members.
test_case.answer: Two engineers on my team disagreed about whether to refactor our auth module before a launch. I scheduled a 45-minute session where each presented their approach with explicit trade-offs on timeline, risk, and long-term maintainability. We ended up shipping the patch first and scheduling the refactor for the next sprint. Both engineers signed off and we launched on time.
test_case.notes: Baseline strong answer with clear vocab overlap to KB — both retrievers should do well.

tfidf__v1_single.feedback: Strong technical example with clear process and outcome. To elevate this answer, add more detail about your facilitation approach and the human dynamics involved in the disagreement.
improvements: Add more context about the initial disagreement dynamics
Explain specific facilitation techniques used during the session
Quantify the impact more precisely (timeline saved, risk mitigated)

tfidf__v2_two_step.feedback: Your impact dimension is weakest at 70/100 because you only mention launching 'on time' without quantifying the business value or measuring the conflict resolution success. Like the strong example that shows 'median review time dropped from 3.5 days to under 1 day,' you should include specific metrics about timeline saved, team productivity gained, or follow-up on whether both engineers remained engaged after the decision.
strengths: Clear structured approach with explicit trade-off analysis
Highly relevant example for engineering management
Good facilitation process that got buy-in from both parties
improvements: Quantify the impact - timeline saved, business value protected, or team satisfaction metrics
Include follow-up results showing the conflict resolution was durable
Specify what 'on time' means with actual dates or sprint goals

semantic__v1_single.feedback: Strong answer with clear process and resolution, but could be enhanced by adding more context about the stakes and quantifying the positive outcomes. Consider explaining why this disagreement mattered to the project and what the results were beyond just 'launched on time.'
improvements: Add context about project importance and timeline pressure
Quantify the impact of the decision (technical debt avoided, time saved, etc.)
Explain how you ensured both engineers felt heard and valued
Mention any follow-up to validate the decision was correct

semantic__v2_two_step.feedback: Your answer lacks the clear STAR structure that makes behavioral responses compelling. While you describe the situation and action well, you don't establish the stakes or context upfront, and you miss quantifying the impact. Compare this to the stakeholder alignment example which opens with clear context ('During a product launch'), shows competing priorities, then delivers a measurable outcome ('Revenue exceeded forecast by 18%').
strengths: Clear conflict resolution process with structured discussion
improvements: Start with clearer context about why this disagreement mattered to the business
Quantify the impact - timeline saved, technical debt avoided, or team dynamics improved
Follow STAR structure more explicitly to make your leadership approach clearer
